serving.natural_language_portion: One large serving of Mala White Bee Hoon is approximately 1200 g total, intended for 4–6 persons sharing, yielding roughly 200–300 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli dish with seafood, estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo component data and USDA FoodData Central reference values for mala-seasoned noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for mala white bee hoon. Values are estimated by component analysis: cooked white rice vermicelli (HPB Foodo: bee hoon, cooked), seafood mix (HPB Foodo: prawns, squid), mala spice paste contribution, egg, and vegetables. Weighted average per 100 g of finished dish calculated from typical recipe proportions for this restaurant-style large-format dish. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice noodles (NDB 20066) and mixed seafood. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 is based on rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (rice vermicelli, GI ~53, low category). Mala seasoning and seafood additions do not substantially alter the GI of the carbohydrate base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 42.3) / 100 ≈ 22.4, based on 291 g per-person portion (1200 g ÷ ~4.1 average persons) containing approximately 42.3 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mala spice paste, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1260 mg per 300 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced sauce or smaller portions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size as the rice vermicelli base contributes a moderate glycemic load (GL ≈ 22 per person serving); pairing with the protein-rich seafood components helps moderate postprandial glucose response, but portion control to approximately 150–200 g per serving is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g); the seafood components provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but the mala sauce and cooking oil contribute to overall cardiovascular risk if consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mala White Bee Hoon (Large) is a Singaporean-Chinese seafood noodle dish featuring white rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked in a bold, numbing-spicy mala sauce made from dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorn, doubanjiang (fermented broad bean paste), garlic, and aromatics. The large format serves 4–6 persons and is a signature dish of The Seafood Market (House of White Bee Hoon) in Singapore. The dish is characterised by its silky, slightly chewy white rice vermicelli strands coated in a rich, reddish-orange mala broth, accompanied by fresh seafood such as prawns, squid, and clams, along with egg and vegetables. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, spicy, and numbing (the characteristic 'má là' sensation from Sichuan peppercorn), with umami depth from oyster sauce and soy sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~118 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from seafood (~7.2 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from the rice vermicelli base (~14.5 g per 100 g). The GI of the rice vermicelli base is approximately 53 (low category), though the glycemic load per person serving is moderate (~22). Sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the mala paste and condiments, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish, mollusc, egg, or soy allergies. It is not halal-certified in its standard form. This dish is popular as a communal sharing meal in Singapore and is associated with the broader trend of Sichuan-influenced mala cuisine in Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for mala white bee hoon. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central reference data for cooked rice vermicelli, mixed seafood, and mala seasoning. GI is based on the analogous rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach and the variability inherent in restaurant-prepared mala dishes.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central component data. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with non-hydrogenated cooking oils and seafood ingredients used in this dish. The large format (1200 g total) is designed for 4–6 persons sharing; per-person portion is approximately 200–300 g. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant. The dish contains multiple common allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, egg, and soy. GI category is low (GI = 53 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving calculated at approximately 22.4 based on a 291 g per-person portion.
